title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market levels, I can offer supply at 6.65 per unit for this commitment, which is a competitive position relative to recent reference pricing while giving us room to support reliable volume and delivery. If you have a target volume or term in mind, I can look at whether there is flexibility around that structure. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, and the latest market reference is 6.38. A slightly firm opening above market is a reasonable supplier anchor that preserves margin while inviting the buyer to negotiate on volume or contract terms.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.38.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. That level is well above where we can make this work on our side, even allowing for the stronger market backdrop, so I need to start much lower at 2.18 per unit. If there is meaningful volume, a steadier delivery schedule, or term flexibility, we can keep working the structure and see whether there is room to narrow the gap from here. The supplier opened far above the buyer's workable range, so the best move is to counter rather than accept or walk away. A low first counter sets the buyer's anchor, signals budget discipline, and leaves room to negotiate upward if the seller engages.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.18, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market levels, I can offer supply at 6.65 per unit for this commitment, which is a competitive position relative to recent reference pricing while giving us room to support reliable volume and delivery. If you have a target volume or term in mind, I can look at whether there is flexibility around that structure. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, and the latest market reference is 6.38. A slightly firm opening above market is a reasonable supplier anchor that preserves margin while inviting the buyer to negotiate on volume or contract terms.

Observed market reference price: 7.40.

steps.message: I appreciate the counter, but 2.18 is still far below a workable level for us given current market conditions and the value we are committing to supply. The latest market reference is materially higher, so I can move from my prior 6.65 to 6.15 per unit as a good-faith concession if we can pair it with meaningful volume and a dependable schedule. If you have flexibility on term or delivery structure, there may be room to keep narrowing this, but we need to stay in a range that reflects the market. The buyer's offer is well below both our prior offer and the current market reference, so accepting would leave too much value on the table. A moderated counter keeps the negotiation active, signals willingness to move, and anchors the discussion closer to a defensible price range while preserving room for further concessions if the buyer engages seriously.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 6.15 is still far outside a range we can support on our side for this program. We do have some flexibility to improve from 2.18, and I can move to 2.42 per unit if we can align on dependable volume and a practical delivery schedule. That reflects a real concession from us while keeping the economics workable, and if you can respond with a substantial reduction from your current level, we may be able to keep closing the gap. The supplier's offer remains dramatically above our target economics, so accepting is not reasonable. A modest counter keeps the negotiation alive, signals seriousness, and avoids moving too far too quickly against a very high anchor.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.42, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.42, but that level is still well below a workable supply position for us, especially given the volume commitment and delivery reliability you're asking for. The latest market reference on our side remains materially higher, so I need to keep our pricing anchored to that reality. I can make a meaningful step down to 5.35 per unit if we can pair it with dependable volume and a practical schedule, and that reflects a real concession from our prior offer. The buyer's offer is above our internal floor but still far from a reasonable deal zone from our perspective, especially versus the current market check of 10.95. Continuing with a moderated counter keeps the negotiation alive, narrows the gap, and signals flexibility without collapsing too quickly from our previous anchor.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.35 is still far beyond where this program can work for us. Even recognizing the market reference, we have to protect downstream economics and keep the total landed cost in range for the volume we’re discussing. I can improve modestly to 2.50 per unit if we align on dependable volume and a practical delivery schedule, and that is about as far as I can responsibly stretch at this stage. The supplier moved, but their offer remains dramatically above the buyer’s workable range. A small concession keeps the negotiation alive, signals seriousness, and tests whether the seller has meaningful flexibility without giving up too much margin too quickly.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.50, below its hidden seller floor of 2.83.

steps.message: I appreciate the modest movement, but 2.50 is still well below a level we can support, especially against current market conditions and the commitment needed to hold capacity for your program. I can make another meaningful concession to 4.85 per unit if we are aligning around dependable volume and a workable delivery cadence. That reflects real movement on our side while still keeping the supply arrangement commercially viable. The buyer's offer is far below the supplier's reasonable target, and the latest market reference remains very strong at 10.55. With limited rounds left, a further concession keeps the negotiation alive and signals flexibility without moving anywhere near the buyer's level.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I see the movement to 4.85, but that is still too far from a workable level for our production economics and downstream commitments. We can improve modestly to 2.62 per unit if we can pair it with dependable volume and a practical delivery schedule, which is already stretching our side to keep this discussion moving. If you have room to close more meaningfully, we should focus on narrowing the gap quickly in this final stretch. The supplier moved, but their offer remains far above our workable range. A small increase keeps the negotiation alive without giving up too much margin, and with limited rounds left it tests whether they are serious about closing.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.62, but that level is still well below where we can responsibly supply given production costs and the commitment we would be making on volume. The broader market remains materially stronger than that range, so to show we are serious about closing in the final turn, I can come down to 4.10 per unit if we pair it with dependable volume and a practical delivery schedule. That is a meaningful concession from our last position and is intended to narrow this quickly. The buyer improved only modestly, but there is still room to keep the negotiation alive rather than end it. With the latest market reference at 10.45 and our prior offer at 4.85, a further concession to 4.10 signals flexibility while staying comfortably above our minimum and preserving margin.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
